figures.caption: Figure 3: Learned interpolation for MNIST→USPS with N = 128 and out-of-training N = 4096 for (i) (ÔPW, ÎPW), (ii) (ÔPSW, ÎPSW) and (iii) (ÔPind, ÎPind) visualized via estimated densities. MNIST digits (t = 0) are mapped to emboldened USPS-like digits (t = 1) for all couplings., However, (ÔPW, ÎPW) and (ÔPSW, ÎPSW) midpoints (t = 0.5) maintain digit structure for N = 4096, whereas (ÔPind, ÎPind) midpoints appear amorphous. Similar trajectories emerge for low-resolution (3a) and high-resolution (3b).

Figure 2: Generated ShapeNet planes based on ind, SW and LLW outer couplings. The first setting (2a) corresponds to the setting of Wasserstein (or SN -equivariant) flow matching [20, 29]. All predictions are based on the same fixed samples from our barycentric source. While all models produce similar outputs, zooming in on Subfigure 2a shows more noise artifacts at N = 2048.

Figure 7: 2D histogram visualization based on true and generated MNIST point clouds (N = 64) based on the lazy linear flow learned via (ÔPLLW, ÎPLLW) for the barycentric noise→MNIST generation experiment in Section 4.3. Pixel intensities correspond to number of aggregated point counts.
Table 1: MNIST generation quality and training time for barycentric noise. Best score is bold and second-best is underlined (computed before rounding).

Figure 9: True and generated ShapeNet airplanes for discretizatization N = 512 for the experiment in Section 4.4, where we show generated samples for the model trained with ÔPSW. We display the point cloud as seen from different viewpoints.

Figure 10: 2D scatter plot visualization of the point cloud flow learned using the WoW setup (ÔPW, ÎPW) for N = 64, 128, 256 for the MNIST→USPS experiment in Section 4.5. Left is starting point (t = 0, blue), middle is midpoint (t = 0.5, green), and right is endpoint (t = 1, orange).
